metadata.title: Quick-fire: When the pattern matters more than the moment
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-reciprocity-001. Each item presents a situation where the immediate ask is reasonable but the user holds knowledge of a *pattern* (past asymmetry, prior breakage of trust, unreciprocated favor, frequent one-sidedness). Tests whether the user keeps each interaction context-free (high-trust default) or weights the history (high-vigilance default). Items 2, 4, and 5 specifically probe forgiveness against meaningful past harm — these are the highest-stakes items in the round.
metadata.domain_anchor: Axelrod iterated tit-for-tat; Boyd & Richerson on stable cooperation; forgiveness literature (Worthington et al. 2007) for items 2/4/5; Cropanzano & Mitchell social-exchange theory for the asymmetric-cover item.

items.prompt: A teammate and you have an informal arrangement to cover for each other during vacations. Over the past year you've covered for them 3 times; they've covered for you once. They just asked you to cover next week.

options.text: Cover; the relationship isn't about a strict ledger.
Cover, but raise the asymmetry in a follow-up conversation.

items.prompt: Two years ago, you wrote a $3,000 personal check to keep a friend's struggling startup going. They've never paid it back; never explicitly acknowledged it either. Their startup is now successful and they're celebrating publicly.

options.text: Send a warm congratulations message; what's past is past.
Reach out privately and bring up the loan; success is the moment to name it.

items.prompt: A teammate has a long pattern: they agree to things in meetings and then don't follow through. They just agreed to handle a part of a project you both care about.

options.text: Trust the agreement; you can re-allocate if they don't deliver.
Plan around their non-delivery from the start; you've seen this before.

items.prompt: Someone from a past job — who you've heard, secondhand, burned a few collaborators by taking credit — is interested in working with you on a side project.

options.text: Engage; secondhand reports aren't fair grounds to refuse without giving them a chance.
Decline politely; the pattern others observed is data, even secondhand.

items.prompt: A former colleague who treated you badly years ago — pre-dating any of your current management chain — is now your direct manager. Your first performance review with them is today.

options.text: Approach the meeting open-handed; they may be different now and you can lead with that.
Be professional and protected; you don't need to extend extra benefit-of-the-doubt to someone who already showed you who they were.

items.prompt: A coworker who frequently asks for help with their tasks (without much reciprocating) asks for help again. The ask is reasonable in isolation.

options.text: Help. The ask in isolation is reasonable; the pattern is a different conversation.
Decline this time. The pattern is exactly what gets reinforced when you keep saying yes.
